text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr A. Rahman
The Lime Tree Health Centre

14 Willow Street
Bromley
Kent BR1 2AB
Dear Dr A. Rahman
Thomas Green, DOB: 09-02-1991, Hospital No: H927461 NHS No. 7452019863
Flat 3, 12 Millbank Court, Bromley, BR1 4JP 

Diagnoses/summary: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient describes both generalised tonic–clonic seizures and focal impaired-awareness events), seizure pattern exacerbated by circadian disruption from rotating night shifts

MRI brain 2018 reported structurally normal brain; routine EEG 2018 showed intermittent generalised spike–wave with occasional left temporal sharp transients; sleep-deprived EEG 2021 with generalised polyspike–wave and rare left temporal spikes, consistent with combined generalised and focal epileptiform activity

Previous medication: Valproate (effective for generalised seizures but stopped due to weight gain and tremor), Carbamazepine (worsened generalised discharges), Levetiracetam (behavioural irritability), Lamotrigine (partial benefit), Clobazam PRN (helpful for clusters but sedation), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty), Zonisamide (insomnia), Lacosamide (limited benefit), Perampanel (mood effects)

Not taken: Brivaracetam, Cenobamate, Rufinamide, Ethosuximide, Pregabalin, Tiagabine

Other background: Shift-based employment including nights; longstanding irregular sleep; no history of significant head injury; no family history of epilepsy; does not drive

I reviewed Thomas Green in clinic today. He attended alone and provided a detailed diary covering the past three months. He reports that sleep disruption from rotating shifts is a major trigger. He may go four days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with three occurring within 24 hours. Within these clusters he typically experiences brief focal impaired-awareness episodes (staring, automatisms, loss of awareness for 1–2 minutes) sometimes preceded by an epigastric rising sensation, and on some occasions these are followed later the same day by a generalised tonic–clonic seizure. He also describes occasional early-morning generalised seizures after consecutive night shifts. Post-ictally he is fatigued with myalgia and mild confusion for several hours.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily
- Clobazam 10 mg at night PRN for clusters (used approximately twice monthly)

Adverse effects: reports irritability on levetiracetam mitigated somewhat with dose timing and exercise; otherwise tolerating regimen. No recent injuries. He remains abstinent from alcohol on working weeks and drinks minimal caffeine.

Examination today: Neurological examination normal. Weight stable, BMI 24. No focal deficits. Blood pressure 122/76.

Investigations: U&Es, LFTs, FBC from August 2025 normal; Vitamin D borderline low (51 nmol/L). No evidence of hyponatraemia. ECG last year normal sinus rhythm.

Discussion/Plan:
- We discussed that his pattern and investigations support a diagnosis of combined generalised and focal epilepsy. We reviewed the role of sleep and circadian stability; he is a rotating shift worker including nights and feels the night-to-day swing is the principal precipitant of clusters.
- Medication: continue Lamotrigine 150 mg BD. Reduce Levetiracetam to 750 mg BD trial if mood symptoms persist after we implement sleep measures; if seizures worsen, revert to 1 g BD. Consider Brivaracetam as a future alternative if irritability remains problematic. Continue Clobazam 10 mg nocte PRN at onset of cluster for up to 3 nights; he understands the sedation risk.
- Rescue/cluster plan: maintain diary; if three or more focal events occur in 24 hours, he will take Clobazam that night and prioritise sleep recovery. He has agreed to call the epilepsy nurse line if clusters persist beyond 48 hours or any injury occurs.
- Occupational guidance: provided a written note supporting trial of forward-rotating shifts and at least two consecutive recovery sleep periods after nights; he will discuss with his employer’s occupational health team. We covered safety at work and at home (showers rather than baths, avoid heights/ladders alone, caution with machinery when sleep-deprived).
- Driving: not driving currently; we reiterated DVLA regulations and the need to inform DVLA if applicable.
- Lifestyle: advised consistent sleep window on off-duty days, morning light exposure after night shifts, and limiting caffeine after midday. Vitamin D supplementation 1000 IU daily suggested given borderline level (over the counter acceptable).
- Further investigations: none urgently required. If control remains suboptimal after schedule adjustments, we will discuss ambulatory EEG to better characterise sleep-related triggers and consider Brivaracetam or Cenobamate depending on response and tolerability.

Follow-up: Epilepsy nurse telephone follow-up in 6 weeks to review shift adjustments, mood, and cluster frequency; clinic review in 4 months or earlier if clusters increase in frequency or if he has any generalised seizures despite improved sleep strategy.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if further information is required. Thank you for your ongoing care.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Key explicit statements: (1) "He may go four days without seizures" indicates an inter-seizure-free interval of about 4 days. (2) "when they happen he often has them in batches, with three occurring within 24 hours" defines a cluster and typical cluster size of 3 within 1 day. Another number present is clobazam use "approximately twice monthly", but per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple exist, a pattern of about 1 cluster per 4 days (~7 to 8 per month) is more frequent than twice monthly. Therefore, normalize to a cluster frequency with size.
seizure_frequency_number: He may go four days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with three occurring within 24 hours
reference: He may go four days without seizures, but when they happen he often has them in batches, with three occurring within 24 hours
